content: Framing ASMR (Agentic search and memory retrieval) systems as purpose-built biological/chemical agents reveals that effective autonomous information retrieval behaves like a targeted pathogenic agent: it actively navigates a host environment (the knowledge graph or external dataset) to exert its specific effect (locating and activating the correct memory for a downstream task), rather than passively matching query terms. This perspective encourages rethinking retrieval system design to prioritize active environmental adaptation over static indexing.
